title: Differentiate products
description: Find the derivatives of products of basic functions.
topic_path: Differentiation: definition and basic derivative rules

hints:
$f(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\cos(x)$ and $x^{-2}$.

Therefore, the derivative of $f$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$f'(x)=-\sin(x)x^{-2}-2\cos(x)x^{-3}$

or any other equivalent form.

$g(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sqrt{x}$ and $\sin(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of $g$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$g'(x)=\dfrac{\sin(x)}{2\sqrt{x}}+\sqrt{x}\cos(x)$

or any other equivalent form.

$\cos(x)x^2$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\cos(x)$ and $x^2$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\cos(x)x^2\right]=2x\cos(x)-x^2\sin(x)$

or any other equivalent form.

$x^3\sin(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $x^3$ and $\sin(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(x^3\sin(x)\right)=x^2(3\sin(x)+x\cos(x))$

or any other equivalent form.

$\cos(x)x^3$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\cos(x)$ and $x^3$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2\cos(x)-x^3\sin(x)$

or any other equivalent form.

$\dfrac1x\sin(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\dfrac1x$ and $\sin(x)$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\cos(x)}{x}-\dfrac{\sin(x)}{x^2}$

or any other equivalent form.

$f(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $x^2$ and $e^x$.

Therefore, the derivative of $f$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$f'(x)= xe^x(x+2)$

or any other equivalent form.

$h(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\ln(x)$ and $\cos(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of $h$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$h'(x)=\dfrac{\cos(x)}{x}-\ln(x)\sin(x)$

or any other equivalent form.

$\dfrac5x \ln(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\dfrac5x$ and $\ln(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac5x \ln(x)\right)=\dfrac{5-5\ln(x)}{x^2}$

or any other equivalent form.

$e^x\cos(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $e^x$ and $\cos(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(e^x\cos(x)\right)=e^x(\cos(x)-\sin(x))$

or any other equivalent form.

$x^2\ln(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $x^2$ and $\ln(x)$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2x\ln(x)+x$

or any other equivalent form.

$\sqrt{x}e^x$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sqrt{x}$ and $e^x$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^x\left(\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}+\sqrt{x}\right)$

or any other equivalent form.

$f(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sin(x)$ and $x^{-2}$.

Therefore, the derivative of $f$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$f'(x)=\dfrac{\cos(x)}{x^2}-\dfrac{2\sin(x)}{x^3}$

$g(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $x$ and $\cos(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of $g$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$g'(x)=\cos(x)-x\sin(x)$

$\dfrac1x\cos(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\dfrac1x$ and $\cos(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac1x\cos(x)\right)=\dfrac{-\cos(x)-x\sin(x)}{x^2}$

$\sin(x)x^2$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sin(x)$ and $x^2$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\sin(x)x^2\right]=x^2\cos(x)+2x\sin(x)$

$\sqrt{x}\cos(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sqrt{x}$ and $\cos(x)$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\cos(x)}{2\sqrt{x}}-\sqrt{x}\sin(x)$

$\sin(x)\cos(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sin(x)$ and $\cos(x)$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\cos^2(x)-\sin^2(x)$

$f(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\dfrac1x$ and $e^x$.

Therefore, the derivative of $f$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$f'(x)=e^x\left(\dfrac1x-\dfrac{1}{x^2}\right)$

$h(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\sqrt{x}$ and $\ln(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of $h$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$h'(x)=\dfrac{\ln(x)}{2\sqrt{x}}+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{x}}$

$\ln(x)e^x$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $\ln(x)$ and $e^x$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln(x)e^x\right)=e^x\left(\dfrac1x+\ln(x)\right)$

$e^x\sin(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $e^x$ and $\sin(x)$.

Therefore, the derivative of the expression can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(e^x\sin(x)\right)=e^x(\sin(x)+\cos(x))$

$x^4\ln(x)$ is the product of two, more basic, expressions: $x^4$ and $\ln(x)$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ can be found using **the product rule**:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)v(x)]&=\dfrac{d}{dx}[u(x)]v(x)+u(x)\dfrac{d}{dx}[v(x)]
\\\\
&=u'(x)v(x)+u(x)v'(x)
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x^3(4\ln(x)+1)$
